content:
4 min read
Just now

--

There’s a running joke in programming circles that every problem eventually gets solved twice — once properly, and once “quickly, in Python.” It’s not really a criticism anymore. Python has quietly become the default starting point for an enormous share of modern software, not because it’s the fastest or the most elegant, but because it gets out of the way faster than almost anything else. Three decades after its creation, it’s still climbing.
The Undisputed King of AI
If there’s one place Python’s dominance is beyond dispute, it’s artificial intelligence. Nearly every major breakthrough in modern machine learning has Python sitting underneath it. PyTorch, the framework behind much of today’s cutting-edge research and production AI, is built Python-first — researchers prototype in it, and increasingly, production systems run on it too. TensorFlow, Google’s answer to the same problem, took a different architectural path but arrived at the same conclusion: if you want AI researchers and engineers to actually use your tools, you write the interface in Python.
This isn’t an accident of popularity — it’s structural. Python’s syntax is close enough to plain English that a data scientist without a formal computer science background can write working code within days. Combine that with libraries like NumPy and pandas for crunching numbers, and scikit-learn for classical machine learning, and you get an ecosystem where the hard part of AI — the math and the ideas — doesn’t have to fight the language for attention. When a new architecture or training technique appears in a research paper, the reference implementation is almost always Python, and that gravitational pull keeps reinforcing itself: more researchers means more tools, more tools means more researchers.
Web & APIs
Python’s second home is the web, and here it’s less about raw research and more about getting real products shipped. Django has been the heavyweight for over fifteen years — a “batteries included” framework that hands you authentication, an admin panel, and an ORM (a tool that lets you interact with a database using Python instead of raw SQL) practically out of the box. It’s the framework of choice when you want to move fast without gluing together a dozen separate libraries yourself.
FastAPI represents the newer wave. Built around Python’s type hints, it’s designed specifically for building APIs and microservices — the small, focused backend services that modern apps are increasingly built from. It’s fast, it auto-generates interactive documentation for your API, and it plays exceptionally well with the async programming Python has been steadily improving over the last several versions. For teams building AI-powered products, FastAPI has become something of a default: it’s often the thin, fast layer that sits between a trained model and the outside world.
Automation
Underneath the flashier use cases, Python still does the unglamorous, essential work it was always good at: automation. Renaming a few thousand files, scraping data off a website, stitching together a report from three different spreadsheets, running a scheduled job that checks something at 3 a.m. so a person doesn’t have to — this is where Python quietly saves people hours every week. Libraries like requests and BeautifulSoup make web scraping almost conversational, and the standard library alone can handle most simple scripting needs without installing anything at all.
It’s worth noting that not everyone loves Python for this. There’s a genuine, ongoing debate among developers — plenty of threads on Reddit and elsewhere gently roast Python for being “clunky” for truly simple, throwaway scripts, arguing that a one-liner in bash or a quick Perl script sometimes gets the job done with less ceremony. It’s a fair point, and it says something honest about Python: it’s rarely the single best tool for any one narrow job. Its real strength is being good enough at nearly everything that you don’t need five different languages installed to get through your day.
The Pros
Python’s biggest strength is readability. Code that reads almost like pseudocode is easier to review, easier to hand off, and easier to come back to six months later. That readability, paired with an enormous ecosystem of well-maintained libraries, means most problems already have a solid, battle-tested package waiting to be imported. Add to that a genuinely massive community — meaning fast answers, abundant tutorials, and a deep hiring pool for companies — and you get a language that’s as good for beginners as it is for large engineering teams.
The Cons
The tradeoffs are real, though. Python is slow compared to compiled languages like C++ or Rust — its dynamic typing and interpreted execution add overhead that becomes a genuine bottleneck for CPU-heavy tasks, which is part of why performance-critical AI code often calls out to C++ under the hood. For years, the Global Interpreter Lock also made true multi-threaded parallelism awkward, forcing developers toward multiprocessing or async workarounds instead of straightforward threads (recent Python versions have started chipping away at this restriction, but it’s not fully gone). And precisely because it’s so flexible, large Python codebases can develop into tangled, hard-to-maintain messes if a team isn’t disciplined about structure and typing.
None of that has slowed it down much. Python’s bet has always been that developer time is more expensive than compute time — and in an era where AI is devouring both, that bet keeps paying off.
